name: Tantalum
name_origin: Named after Tantalus, a figure from Greek mythology, by Anders Ekeberg in 1802 to reflect the element's inability to absorb acid (alluding to Tantalus' inability to drink the water he stood in).

occurrence.abundance_summary: Tantalum is rare, occurring at about 1-2 ppm in Earth's crust. It is found primarily in the mineral tantalite and almost always coexists with niobium in columbite-tantalite (coltan) ores, with major production in Australia, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Brazil, and Rwanda.

compounds.name: Tantalum pentoxide
compounds.summary: A high-permittivity dielectric used in capacitors, anti-reflection coatings, and as a precursor to tantalum metal.

compounds.name: Tantalum carbide
compounds.summary: An extremely hard refractory ceramic (m.p. ~3880 C) used in cutting-tool inserts and rocket nozzle linings.

compounds.name: Tantalum pentachloride
compounds.summary: A volatile yellow solid used as a precursor in chemical vapor deposition of Ta and Ta-based films.

compounds.name: Lithium tantalate
compounds.summary: A piezoelectric and pyroelectric crystal used in surface-acoustic-wave (SAW) filters and electro-optic modulators.

compounds.name: Tantalum nitride
compounds.summary: A hard, chemically stable ceramic used as a thin-film resistor material and as a copper diffusion barrier in chip interconnects.

uses: Tantalum electrolytic capacitors in mobile phones, laptops, and other compact electronics
Surgical implants (bone plates, cranial mesh) due to its biocompatibility and inertness
Corrosion-resistant chemical process equipment (heat exchangers, reactor liners)
Superalloys for jet engine and gas turbine components
Carbide cutting tools and refractory ceramics
Sputtering targets for thin-film barrier layers in semiconductors
reactivity_summary: Tantalum is a dense, ductile, blue-gray metal that is exceptionally corrosion resistant below 150 C, attacked only by hydrofluoric acid, fuming sulfuric acid, fluorine gas, and molten alkalis. It forms a tenacious Ta2O5 passivation layer that protects bulk metal from virtually all aqueous chemistry. At higher temperatures it reacts readily with oxygen, the halogens, carbon, and nitrogen.
isotopes_summary: Tantalum has two naturally occurring isotopes: 181Ta (~99.988%, stable) and 180mTa, a nuclear isomer that is the rarest primordial nuclide and the only naturally occurring nuclear isomer (apparent half-life > 4.5x10^16 yr). The ground-state 180Ta is unstable. Synthetic isotopes from 155Ta to 190Ta have been produced for research.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
